name: The Tarot of All Tarots — Five Centuries of the Cards
description:
A meta-tarot holding five public domain decks in dialogue across 78 cards. Each card is a prism: the same archetype seen through the Rider-Waite-Smith (1909), Tarot de Marseille (1760), Sola Busca (1491), three editions of Etteilla (1788/1838/1865), and the Bolognese Tarocchino. Every card includes contextual readings for professional, relationship, and consciousness questions, plus a shadow reading.

The deck images render inline — you see Colman Smith's golden Fool beside Conver's woodcut Le Mat beside the Sola Busca's wild-haired Mato, each carrying the same archetype through different centuries and sensibilities. The three Etteilla editions show how a single deck evolved across 77 years: from Basan's austere engravings (1788) through Blocquel's recycled letterpress (1838) to Regamey's vibrant chromolithographs (1865).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Pamela Colman Smith's original 1909 RWS illustrations (Wikimedia Commons), Nicolas Conver's 1760 Marseille woodcuts (Gallica/BnF via Wikimedia), Sola Busca engravings c. 1491 (Pinacoteca di Brera via Wikimedia), Etteilla I Basan engravings 1788 (Benebell Wen reconstruction), Etteilla II Blocquel letterpress 1838, Etteilla III Regamey chromolithographs 1865, Tarocchino Arlecchino by Yve Lepkowski 2022.

sections.Archetype: The eternal beginner. Zero contains everything and nothing — the void before creation, the leap before the landing. Every journey begins with the Fool's willingness to step into the unknown without a map. This is not ignorance but a radical openness that the experienced have forgotten.
sections.Professional: You are being called to begin something without guarantee of success. The Fool in career readings says: take the leap. Start the business, change the field, propose the idea that scares you. Your lack of experience is actually your advantage — you'll see what the experts can't.
sections.Relationship: Fall in love with the falling. The Fool brings the energy of new romance — exhilarating, terrifying, completely irrational. In established relationships, it asks: where have you stopped being surprised? Bring beginner's mind back to someone you think you know.
sections.Consciousness: The Fool is the consciousness before it identifies with any role or story. In meditation, this is the space between thoughts. In spiritual life, it's the willingness to abandon everything you think you know about enlightenment and just... walk.
sections.Shadow: Recklessness disguised as freedom. Refusing to learn from experience. Serial beginning without completion. The spiritual tourist who collects initiations but never commits to practice. Naivety weaponized against those asking reasonable questions.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_00_Fool.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes innocence and new beginnings – a free spirit embarking on the unknown.

**Upright:** Upright, The Fool represents innocence, new beginnings and free spirit. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to embrace these qualities, using them as a source of strength and motivation for personal growth.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The Fool may signal recklessness, taken advantage of and inconsideration. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to reflect on underlying causes and beliefs contributing to such impulsive behavior, so you can make more mindful choices going forward.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/LE_MAT_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Starting without certainty—choosing curiosity over control.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** A new chapter wants a lighter grip. Notice the urge to predict outcomes; try a small, values-aligned experiment instead. Ask what you would do if you were allowed to be a beginner.

**Reversed:** Impulsivity or avoidance. If you’re calling it “freedom,” check whether it’s actually escape. Slow down: name the real fear, then choose one responsible next step.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Mato](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2000.jpg)

**Card name:** Mato

**Etymology:** The Fool / Madman. From Italian 'matto' meaning mad, foolish, or insane. The only unnumbered trump in most early tarot decks; here numbered 0

**Historical figure:** The figure appears to depict a Celt or barbarian, distinct from the Roman figures on other trumps

**Historical context:** The Mato card of the Sola Busca tarot stands apart from the other trumps, which predominantly feature Roman historical figures. The wild-haired figure dressed in simple garments represents the archetypal outsider—the one who exists beyond the boundaries of civilized Roman society. In Renaissance Italy, the 'matto' held a complex social position: sometimes revered as touched by divine madness, other times feared as dangerous. The card's placement as zero reflects both nothing and infinite potential—the void from which all creation springs.

The imagery draws on medieval traditions of the court fool, who alone could speak truth to power without consequence. The Sola Busca's Mato carries a staff and appears mid-journey, suggesting the eternal wanderer who has renounced worldly attachments. Some scholars connect this figure to the Celtic warriors Rome encountered at the edges of empire—fierce, unpredictable, and operating by entirely different rules.

The card's symbolism speaks to beginnings that require abandoning certainty. Unlike the named Roman figures who represent specific historical lessons, Mato represents the universal human capacity for fresh starts and the courage to step into the unknown without a map.

**Symbolism:** beginning, innocence, spontaneity, free spirit, divine madness, the outsider
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):
**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

![Etteilla I — La Folie (The Questioner - Female)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1yokAdARGRincfoSYVc8vaBBOrZR8ZSl-)

**Meaning:** The questioning spirit—openness to the unknown.

**Reversed:** Closed-mindedness or reckless questioning. Either not truly open to answers, or asking without being ready for truth.



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1oSBgNrF4033lNRBj_M6NeWY2HK0vU_oY)

*Changes from Edition I: Basan's original engravings replaced with letterpress reproductions. Lower panels redesigned with recycled imagery. Margin titles added to all cards. Published 47 years after Etteilla's death under the pseudonym 'Julia Orsini.'*



**EDITION III — Oracle des Dames (1865, Regamey chromolithographs)**

![Etteilla III](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1CHzF_JbH5glqYWmH9x4oNRSg3tKVIa8e)

*Changes from Editions I/II: First colored Etteilla deck. Vibrant chromolithography by G. Regamey for Haugard-Mauge. Marketed as 'Oracle des Dames' (Ladies' Oracle) for middle-class parlor entertainment. Preserves Genesis ordering and Hermetic symbolism in a more visually polished format.*
sections.Etteilla Genesis Tradition:
*In Etteilla's Genesis-based ordering, this position corresponds to Le Chaos (The Questioner - Male) — a card unique to the Etteilla tradition with no direct RWS equivalent.*



**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

![Etteilla I — Le Chaos (The Questioner - Male)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1tN3bDQnjpbXs46DOM3UVJOmTRnR7FqM-)

**French name:** Le Chaos

**Meaning:** The primordial void before creation—potential awaiting form.

**Reversed:** Confusion without purpose. The void feels empty rather than full of potential. Ground yourself before seeking answers.

**Hermetic correspondence:** Corresponds to the Ein Sof in Kabbalah, the Tao in Eastern philosophy, and the prima materia in alchemy

**Genesis connection:** Before Creation - The Void



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1yZmpYLHMkv7LL7SOmCcu3MP6A5A4q5Xb)

*Changes from Edition I: Basan's original engravings replaced with letterpress reproductions. Lower panels redesigned with recycled imagery. Margin titles added to all cards. Published 47 years after Etteilla's death under the pseudonym 'Julia Orsini.'*



**EDITION III — Oracle des Dames (1865, Regamey chromolithographs)**

![Etteilla III](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1YBXHzeDRCSyDaIiHHqZhvLs1OANyGaO9)

*Changes from Editions I/II: First colored Etteilla deck. Vibrant chromolithography by G. Regamey for Haugard-Mauge. Marketed as 'Oracle des Dames' (Ladies' Oracle) for middle-class parlor entertainment. Preserves Genesis ordering and Hermetic symbolism in a more visually polished format.*
sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — La Folie (The Fool)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1HoJfCJ_0V__rDvYizi-eMt0STbLKlM_7)

**Bolognese name:** La Folie (The Fool)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** The Fool marching with drum and horn, representing madness, folly, or empty negligence.

**Interpretation:** Madness, insanity, insane, eccentricity, enthusiasm, unreasonableness, blindness, ignorance, senseless, confusion, wandering, drunkenness, delirium, fever, frenzy, rage, passion, fury, transport, innocent, simple, silly.

**Reversed:** Imbecility, ineptitude, insouciance, stupidity, imprudence, negligence, absence, absent-mindedness, apathy, nullity, empty, nothing, useless.

sections.Archetype: The channel between above and below, the one who makes the invisible visible. The Magician doesn't create from nothing — they arrange what's already present into new configurations. One hand points to heaven, the other to earth: 'As above, so below.' This is the archetype of conscious creation through focused will.
sections.Professional: You have all the tools you need — they're on the table in front of you. The Magician says: stop gathering resources and start combining them. Communication, initiative, and skill converge now. A powerful time for pitching, presenting, or launching.
sections.Relationship: Conscious communication transforms a relationship. The Magician brings charisma and intentionality — the power to create the dynamic you want through clear speech and focused attention. But ensure you're creating WITH someone, not performing AT them.
sections.Consciousness: The awakened will. The realization that consciousness is not passive — it actively shapes reality through attention and intention. This is the archetype behind every meditation practice, every prayer, every act of deliberate creation.
sections.Shadow: Manipulation. Using charisma and skill to deceive. The con artist, the smooth talker, the guru who uses spiritual language to control. Power without ethics. Technique without heart.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_01_Magician.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes willpower, desire, and the ability to manifest one’s intentions into reality.

**Upright:** Upright, The Magician represents willpower, desire, creation and manifestation. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to tap into your personal power and focus your intentions, recognizing that you have the tools to create positive change in your life.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The Magician may signal trickery, illusions and being out of touch. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to examine where you may be deceiving yourself or feeling powerless, so you can ground yourself in honesty and regain a sense of control.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/I_LE_BATELEUR_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** You have enough to begin—focus converts intention into action.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Clarify one goal and one next action. The mind may be overthinking; return to what is controllable: attention, effort, and practice. Choose a simple plan and execute.

**Reversed:** Scattered effort or self-deception (“I can’t” / “it’s pointless”). Identify the distortion, then pick a 10-minute proof-of-ability action.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Panfilio](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2001.jpg)

**Card name:** Panfilio

**Etymology:** All-loving / Friend of all. From Greek 'pan' (all) + 'philos' (loving/friend). Often interpreted as 'The Popular Man' or 'The Social Man'

**Historical figure:** Possibly Boccaccio's Panfilo from the Decameron, or a symbolic 'everyman' figure

**Historical context:** Panfilio embodies the archetype of one who possesses both knowledge and the skill to apply it. The name's Greek etymology—'friend to all'—suggests a figure who bridges different worlds through wisdom and social grace. In Boccaccio's Decameron, Panfilo is described as one who 'repeatedly emphasizes the need to look deeper into stories,' presenting 'characters and situations which hide their true nature.'

This connection to deeper seeing aligns perfectly with the traditional Magician archetype. The Sola Busca depicts Panfilio as a figure of learning and magical knowledge—one who understands that true mastery lies not in domination but in harmonious relationship with all things. The Greek roots of the name suggest Hermetic and Neoplatonic influences prevalent in Renaissance thought.

Panfilio represents the conscious will directed skillfully. Like the Magician of later decks who stands with one hand pointing to heaven and one to earth, Panfilio embodies the principle of 'as above, so below'—the wise figure who channels higher knowledge into practical manifestation through friendship with all levels of existence.

**Symbolism:** mastery, skill, mediation, wisdom applied, social connection, magical knowledge
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):
**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

![Etteilla I — Le Magicien ou le Bateleur (The Magician/Juggler)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1pbbzJ3OjACrI4huDbuLvyuoql2NpNxH3)

**Meaning:** Skill, craft, and the power to manifest through practiced art.

**Reversed:** Misuse of abilities or blocked manifestation. Either using your skills for wrong purposes, or not trusting your capacity to create. Trickery or deception.



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1orx1nqjVLulUNx1R3qrj1TqHSBs9L9Up)

*Changes from Edition I: Basan's original engravings replaced with letterpress reproductions. Lower panels redesigned with recycled imagery. Margin titles added to all cards. Published 47 years after Etteilla's death under the pseudonym 'Julia Orsini.'*



**EDITION III — Oracle des Dames (1865, Regamey chromolithographs)**

![Etteilla III](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1S64FZ5r07LBxBM9mhHfmfry-Oq4nyC4W)

*Changes from Editions I/II: First colored Etteilla deck. Vibrant chromolithography by G. Regamey for Haugard-Mauge. Marketed as 'Oracle des Dames' (Ladies' Oracle) for middle-class parlor entertainment. Preserves Genesis ordering and Hermetic symbolism in a more visually polished format.*
sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — Le Bateleur (The Magician)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1eLd6Z1kWTfFJBpvPVa1adASBydp1ZX5p)

**Bolognese name:** Le Bateleur (The Magician)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** The Magician as trickster, surrounded by impish figures, representing illness and deception.

**Interpretation:** Illness of the body, disease, physical affliction, infirmity, damage, bad state of health or affairs, disruption, pain, death throes, epidemic, poison, plague, infection.

**Reversed:** Illness of the mind or soul, indisposition, headache, heartache, worry, melancholy, affliction, lethargy, inconvenience, unhappy position, displeasure, anxiety, sorrow, woe, misfortune, medicine, remedy, charlatan, mage, empirical medicine.

sections.Archetype: The keeper of hidden knowledge. She sits between the pillars of light and dark, holding a scroll she doesn't show you — not because you can't see it, but because some truths must be approached through silence. The High Priestess is intuition before it becomes thought, knowing before it becomes words.
sections.Professional: Trust your gut. The data is incomplete, the rational analysis falls short, but something in you knows. The High Priestess in career matters says: listen to what isn't being said in the meeting, read between the lines of the contract, pay attention to the unease you can't explain.
sections.Relationship: The mystery at the heart of every relationship — the parts of your partner you'll never fully know, and the parts of yourself that emerge only in their presence. Honor the unknown. Stop trying to make everything explicit.
sections.Consciousness: Pure receptivity. The consciousness that receives rather than grasps. In meditation, this is the awareness that witnesses thoughts without engaging them. The lunar mind, the dream life, the voice that speaks in symbols.
sections.Shadow: Secrets that become lies. Withholding as power. Using mystery to avoid intimacy. The spiritual bypasser who retreats into 'inner knowing' to avoid engaging with messy human reality.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_02_High_Priestess.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes intuition and the unconscious – the inner voice and hidden knowledge within.

**Upright:** Upright, The High Priestess represents intuition, the unconscious and one’s inner voice. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to trust your gut feelings and pay attention to the subtle messages from your inner self, acknowledging the wisdom that comes from within.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The High Priestess may signal a lack of center, lost inner voice or repressed feelings. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to explore what distractions or anxieties are drowning out your intuition, and to create space for your true feelings to surface and be understood.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/II_LA_PAPESSE_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Listening beneath the noise—your body and dreams carry data.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Before problem-solving, gather signals: feelings, sensations, patterns. Let the answer emerge by observing rather than forcing. Journal: what do I already know but don’t want to admit?

**Reversed:** Second-guessing or numbness. If you feel “nothing,” check for overload. Reduce input, restore sleep, and ask for one gentle truth at a time.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Postumio](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2002.jpg)

**Card name:** Postumio

**Etymology:** Born after / The Posthumous One. From Latin 'postumus' meaning last, born after the father's death. References the Roman gens Postumia

**Historical figure:** Likely Aulus Postumius Albinus or another member of the Postumii family from the Roman Republic

**Historical context:** The Postumii were one of the most ancient and distinguished patrician families of Rome, with members serving as consuls, dictators, and military commanders throughout the Republic. The name 'Postumus' originally meant 'last' but came to mean 'born after the father's death,' carrying connotations of hidden origins and knowledge passed through generations.

In the context of the Sola Busca, Postumio represents hidden authority and inherited wisdom—that which is received from those who came before. The figure corresponds to the High Priestess archetype, guardian of mysteries that lie between the conscious and unconscious realms. This is knowledge that cannot be spoken directly but must be intuited.

Researchers have connected Postumio to 'POSTUMIUS' mentioned in Book 5 of Paulus Orosius's 'Historiarum Adversum Paganos,' suggesting the deck's creator drew from this ancient historical compendium. The Postumii's role in Roman religion and their connection to foundational myths of Rome reinforces the card's association with secret knowledge and the transmission of sacred traditions across time.

**Symbolism:** mystery, hidden knowledge, inheritance, intuition, the veil between worlds
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):
*In Etteilla's Genesis-based ordering, this position corresponds to La Lumière (Light/Enlightenment) — a card unique to the Etteilla tradition with no direct RWS equivalent.*



**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

![Etteilla I — La Lumière (Light/Enlightenment)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1xJkxGpUJGR3qOh0UDb-tkjkhMNg1l40K)

**French name:** La Lumière

**Meaning:** The first act of creation—light emerges from darkness.

**Reversed:** Denial or willful blindness. The truth is available but you may be avoiding it. What uncomfortable clarity are you resisting?

**Hermetic correspondence:** The Divine Nous (Mind) of Hermetic philosophy; the first emanation of the Absolute

**Genesis connection:** Day 1 - Creation of Light



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1wxTDm3uaUOSH_4hrg0s851qcflnoiqvh)

*Changes from Edition I: Basan's original engravings replaced with letterpress reproductions. Lower panels redesigned with recycled imagery. Margin titles added to all cards. Published 47 years after Etteilla's death under the pseudonym 'Julia Orsini.'*



**EDITION III — Oracle des Dames (1865, Regamey chromolithographs)**

![Etteilla III](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1V9WW2sOg0LTM5XaRLHfIDd3_gjTDzQX7)

*Changes from Editions I/II: First colored Etteilla deck. Vibrant chromolithography by G. Regamey for Haugard-Mauge. Marketed as 'Oracle des Dames' (Ladies' Oracle) for middle-class parlor entertainment. Preserves Genesis ordering and Hermetic symbolism in a more visually polished format.*
sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — La Papesse (The High Priestess)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1PI4LVQ6vWTaBQgPm47k66-0zpc-J4m9E)

**Bolognese name:** La Papesse (The High Priestess)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** Harlequin as Pope Joan, representing rest, solitude, and disguise.

**Interpretation:** Rest, tranquility, quiet, retirement, withdrawal, private life, secret life, life of solitude, hermitage, religious life, silence, taciturnity, composure.

**Reversed:** Imitation, disguise, image, impersonation, pseudonym, mimicry, parody, camouflage, illusion, reflection, mirroring, replica, simulacrum, counterpart, counterfeit, apocryphal.

sections.Archetype: The Great Mother — not domesticated, not gentle, but fecund and wild. The Empress is nature's intelligence expressing through you: the body's wisdom, the creative impulse, the urge to nurture what you've brought into being. She is abundance without apology.
sections.Professional: A fertile period for creative work. Projects grow naturally now — nurture them with attention but don't over-manage. The Empress in career says: create beauty, tend to the workplace environment, lead through abundance rather than scarcity.
sections.Relationship: Sensual, nurturing, deeply embodied love. The Empress brings physical affection, domestic warmth, and the creation of beauty in shared spaces. If you want to improve a relationship, cook together, garden together, make something together.
sections.Consciousness: The body as sacred temple. The Empress is embodied spirituality — not transcendence but immanence. God is not above; God is in the soil, the bread, the lover's skin. Practice: notice where your body is right now.
sections.Shadow: Smothering. Over-identifying with the nurturer role. Creative overproduction without discernment. Using beauty or pleasure to avoid necessary pain. The devouring mother.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_03_Empress.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes abundance, fertility, and nurturing – the creative, motherly energy of growth and prosperity.

**Upright:** Upright, The Empress represents motherhood, fertility and nature. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to nurture yourself and others, fostering creativity and allowing abundance to grow in your life by caring for your physical and emotional needs.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The Empress may signal dependence, smothering, emptiness or nosiness. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to consider where you might feel empty or overbearing – and to cultivate healthy independence and self-care, rather than seeking validation solely from others.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/II_L%27IMP%C3%89RATRICE_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** What you nurture grows—start with your own inner ecosystem.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Support the conditions for thriving: rest, food, time, affection, beauty. If you’re stuck, shift from self-critique to self-care and watch motivation return.

**Reversed:** Overgiving, depletion, or creative blockage. Practice boundaries: say no to one drain; say yes to one replenishing act today.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Lenpio](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2003.jpg)

**Card name:** Lenpio

**Etymology:** Possibly 'The Gentle One' or corruption of Lepidus. Possibly from Latin 'lenis' (gentle, mild) or a scribal error for 'Lepidus'. Some scholars suggest this represents Marcus Aemilius Lepidus

**Historical figure:** Likely Marcus Aemilius Lepidus, member of the Second Triumvirate with Octavian and Mark Antony

**Historical context:** If Lenpio represents Lepidus, this card depicts one of the most powerful men in Roman history—a member of the triumvirate that ruled Rome after Caesar's assassination. The Aemilii Lepidi were among the oldest and most aristocratic families of Rome, known for their wealth, cultivation, and patronage of the arts.

The name 'Lepidus' itself means 'charming' or 'pleasant,' connecting to themes of grace, beauty, and abundance. Marcus Aemilius Lepidus served as Pontifex Maximus, Rome's chief priest, giving him religious authority alongside political power. His role as bridge-builder between factions reflects the nurturing, unifying energy of the Empress archetype.

The Sola Busca's third trump embodies generative power and material abundance. Whether representing Lepidus specifically or drawing on the etymological meaning of gentleness, Lenpio speaks to the creative force that brings forth life and sustains it. The connection to Book 5 of Orosius reinforces the card's grounding in historical narrative while pointing toward universal themes of fertility, nurture, and the sustaining power of nature.

**Symbolism:** fertility, abundance, gentleness, nurturing, creative force, material comfort
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):
*In Etteilla's Genesis-based ordering, this position corresponds to Les Plantes (Plants/Growth) — a card unique to the Etteilla tradition with no direct RWS equivalent.*



**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

![Etteilla I — Les Plantes (Plants/Growth)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1WDNMiOd1Cg3PLVB0QhGRLi7LChDo9x7g)

**French name:** Les Plantes

**Meaning:** The emergence of life—growth, fertility, and natural development.

**Reversed:** Stagnation or forced growth. Either something isn't developing naturally, or you're trying to rush what needs time.

**Hermetic correspondence:** The vegetable soul; the life force animating organic matter

**Genesis connection:** Day 3 - Creation of Plants



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1zcIBhh-Xhb25YPv-XfccQNhi9I6k9D90)

*Changes from Edition I: Basan's original engravings replaced with letterpress reproductions. Lower panels redesigned with recycled imagery. Margin titles added to all cards. Published 47 years after Etteilla's death under the pseudonym 'Julia Orsini.'*

![Etteilla III](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1PWimudHamT2KgLCS8LmUn1duOzN3kw7i)

*Changes from Editions I/II: First colored Etteilla deck. Vibrant chromolithography by G. Regamey for Haugard-Mauge. Marketed as 'Oracle des Dames' (Ladies' Oracle) for middle-class parlor entertainment. Preserves Genesis ordering and Hermetic symbolism in a more visually polished format.*
sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — L'Imperatrice (The Empress)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1PRkrrBvMKHT_PXYm-x1zu9yHRQiyWKf9)

**Bolognese name:** L'Imperatrice (The Empress)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** The Elf-king's daughter wielding a spear, representing darkness, mystery, and hidden truths.

**Interpretation:** Night, darkness, eclipse, mist, obscurity, mystery, secret, hidden, unknown, clandestine, occult, parable, allegory, mystical meaning, mask, veil, cover, sheath, envelop, unvoiced, blindness, oblivion, obfuscate.

**Reversed:** Day, light of day, brightness, clarity, truth, visible, enlighten, manifestation, demonstration, evidence, engender, give life to, uncover, bring light to, publish, come out, bloom, emerge, come to light, zodiac.

sections.Archetype: Structure that serves life. The Emperor builds the walls that protect the garden — without him, the Empress's abundance has no form. This is the archetype of benevolent authority: rules that create freedom, boundaries that enable growth, power exercised for the common good.
sections.Professional: Take charge. Build systems. Establish the structure that will sustain your vision long-term. The Emperor in career says: write the business plan, set the budget, hire the team, define the roles. Vision without structure is just daydreaming.
sections.Relationship: Stability, protection, and clear boundaries. The Emperor in relationships provides the structure within which love can safely grow — but must be careful not to become controlling. The question: are your rules protecting the relationship or protecting your ego?
sections.Consciousness: The ordered mind. The capacity to think systematically, to build mental models, to create frameworks for understanding. But consciousness is bigger than any framework — the Emperor must eventually bow to what he can't contain.
sections.Shadow: Tyranny. Rigidity. Control masquerading as protection. The father wound — authority experienced as oppression. Building walls so high that nothing new can enter.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_04_Emperor.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes authority, structure, and control – the fatherly influence of stability and responsibility.

**Upright:** Upright, The Emperor represents authority, structure, control and fatherhood. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to develop healthy boundaries and self-discipline, stepping into your own authority over your life while balancing firmness with compassion.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The Emperor may signal tyranny, rigidity or coldness. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to examine issues with control or inflexibility in your life – possibly a tendency to be too harsh on yourself or others – and to work on introducing understanding and flexibility where it’s needed.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/IIII-L%27EMPEREUR_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Stability is built—clear rules reduce anxiety.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Choose structure that supports your values: schedules, limits, roles, agreements. Where life feels chaotic, create one simple rule and follow it for a week.

**Reversed:** Rigidity or power struggles. If control is the coping strategy, practice flexibility: ask what outcome matters most and loosen everything else.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Mario](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2004.jpg)

**Card name:** Mario

**Etymology:** Of Mars / Martial. From Latin 'Marius,' derived from Mars, the god of war. References the famous Roman general Gaius Marius

**Historical figure:** Gaius Marius (157-86 BCE), Roman general and statesman who reformed the Roman army

**Historical context:** Gaius Marius stands as one of the most consequential figures in Roman history. Rising from humble origins in Arpinum, he transformed Rome's military from a citizen militia into a professional army—a reform that would ultimately contribute to the fall of the Republic as generals gained personal loyalty from their troops.

Marius held the consulship an unprecedented seven times and was called 'the third founder of Rome' for saving the city from Germanic invasions. His rivalry with Sulla led to civil war and proscriptions that traumatized Roman society. The name 'Marius' connects to Mars, god of war, perfectly embodying the martial authority of the Emperor archetype.

In the Sola Busca, Mario represents structural power, military authority, and the establishment of order through strength. The reference to 'MARIUS' in Book 5 of Orosius's history places him within a moral narrative about the rise and fall of great men. Mario/Marius teaches that authority must be wielded wisely—his reforms strengthened Rome, but his political violence showed how power can corrupt even the greatest leaders.

**Symbolism:** authority, structure, military power, reform, order through strength
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):
*In Etteilla's Genesis-based ordering, this position corresponds to Le Ciel (The Heavens) — a card unique to the Etteilla tradition with no direct RWS equivalent.*



**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

**French name:** Le Ciel

**Meaning:** The sky and celestial realm—higher perspective and divine order.

**Reversed:** Lost in details, missing the bigger picture. Anxiety about things beyond your control. Return to trust.

**Hermetic correspondence:** The celestial sphere; the realm of planetary intelligences and fixed stars

**Genesis connection:** Day 2 - Creation of the Firmament



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=151EHI16d_AuiY4_EM8O5FjTXPB_C5d6w)

*Changes from Edition I: Basan's original engravings replaced with letterpress reproductions. Lower panels redesigned with recycled imagery. Margin titles added to all cards. Published 47 years after Etteilla's death under the pseudonym 'Julia Orsini.'*



**EDITION III — Oracle des Dames (1865, Regamey chromolithographs)**

![Etteilla III](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1ldQ_YmdppaFSk9Sd8cxrhMXkjEHKw2uw)

*Changes from Editions I/II: First colored Etteilla deck. Vibrant chromolithography by G. Regamey for Haugard-Mauge. Marketed as 'Oracle des Dames' (Ladies' Oracle) for middle-class parlor entertainment. Preserves Genesis ordering and Hermetic symbolism in a more visually polished format.*
sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — L'Empereur (The Emperor)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1-qHWDIbYrUno6_y8e7--cwgunG8sbhd8)

**Bolognese name:** L'Empereur (The Emperor)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** King Herla with his hound, representing support, foundation, and protection.

**Interpretation:** Support, backing, holding up, column, base, foundation, basis, founding principles, hypothesis, reason, cause, subject, fixity, stability, assurance, persuasion, conviction, confidence, security, reliability, help, aid.

**Reversed:** Protection, defense, influence, benevolence, benefaction, charity, humaneness, goodness, shelter, preservation, aegis.

sections.Archetype: The bridge between the human and the divine — not through private mysticism but through shared tradition. The Hierophant represents teaching, initiation, and the living transmission of wisdom through community. He is the structure of spiritual practice itself.
sections.Professional: Mentorship, institutional knowledge, and established paths. The Hierophant in career says: find a teacher, follow the proven method, respect the tradition before you innovate. Sometimes the conventional path IS the right path.
sections.Relationship: Commitment formalized — marriage, vows, shared spiritual practice. The Hierophant in relationships asks: what is the sacred container holding your love? Shared values, shared rituals, shared community strengthen bonds.
sections.Consciousness: The role of tradition in spiritual growth. Not all structures are prisons — some are ladders. Meditation techniques, prayer practices, initiatory lineages: these are the Hierophant's gifts. The question is whether you're learning from the tradition or hiding behind it.
sections.Shadow: Dogma. Spiritual authority used to control. The institution that protects itself at the expense of the people it serves. Conformity mistaken for belonging. Shame-based religion.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_05_Hierophant.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes tradition, spirituality, and guidance – values, ethics, and the wisdom of collective teachings.

**Upright:** Upright, The Hierophant represents tradition, conformity, morality and ethics. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to explore your belief systems and values – understanding the family or cultural traditions that shaped you – and to seek wise counsel or mentorship when needed.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The Hierophant may signal rebellion, subversiveness or new approaches. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to question outdated beliefs and find your own authentic path. It suggests that breaking from convention can be healthy if your current norms no longer serve your personal growth.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/V_LE_PAPE_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Guidance and shared meaning—learn the rule before you break it.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Name the value beneath the rule. Seek a teacher, model, or method that aligns with your ethics. Practice consistency: small rituals create stability.

**Reversed:** Dogma or rebellion for rebellion’s sake. If you’re rejecting everything, ask what you still need: belonging, purpose, or clarity—and meet it directly.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Catulo](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2005.jpg)

**Card name:** Catulo

**Etymology:** Little dog / Puppy. From Latin 'catulus' meaning puppy or whelp. References the Roman gens Lutatia, specifically Quintus Lutatius Catulus

**Historical figure:** Quintus Lutatius Catulus (150-87 BCE), Roman consul and co-commander with Marius

**Historical context:** Quintus Lutatius Catulus served as consul alongside Gaius Marius during the desperate wars against the Cimbri and Teutones, Germanic tribes threatening Italy itself. Though Marius received most of the glory, Catulus played a crucial role in the final victory at Vercellae in 101 BCE. He was known for his literary refinement and his dedication to traditional Roman values.

Catulus represented the old aristocratic traditions of Rome—piety, learning, and adherence to ancestral customs. His conflict with the populist Marius embodied the fundamental tension in late Republican politics between innovation and tradition. The name's meaning of 'puppy' or 'young dog' may seem diminutive, but in Roman culture it suggested loyalty and the continuation of lineage.

As the fifth trump, Catulo corresponds to the Hierophant—the keeper of tradition and teacher of established wisdom. The reference to 'CATULUS' in Book 5 of Orosius connects this card to themes of cultural transmission and the sometimes violent conflicts over who defines orthodoxy. Catulo reminds us that tradition provides structure and meaning, but must be held with wisdom rather than rigid dogmatism.

**Symbolism:** tradition, orthodoxy, teaching, cultural transmission, loyalty to ancestors

sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — Le Pape (The Hierophant)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1wZuh6KOlaOw_mmTYVOeTAoHgJc5eR3nA)

**Bolognese name:** Le Pape (The Hierophant)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** The Pope with stigmata and sacred book, representing divinity, wisdom, and contemplation.

**Interpretation:** God, spirit of God, all-powerful, eternal, unique, divinity, devotee, faithfulness, worship, master.

**Reversed:** Philosopher, philosophical, philosophize, sage, wisdom, knowledge, tradition, thought, meditation, contemplation.

sections.Archetype: The moment of choice that defines everything after. The Lovers is not merely romance — it's the archetype of conscious choice, of values clarified through the experience of desire. When you choose one path, you release all others. This is the card of commitment born from genuine attraction.
sections.Professional: A significant choice that will define your professional identity. Partnerships, mergers, or value-based decisions. The Lovers in career says: choose what you love, not what's safe. But choose — ambivalence is its own decision.
sections.Relationship: Deep attraction, soul-level connection, and the terrifying vulnerability of truly choosing another person. The Lovers asks: are you choosing this relationship freely, or are you choosing it by default? Real love requires real choice.
sections.Consciousness: The union of opposites within the self — masculine and feminine, conscious and unconscious, human and divine. The alchemical marriage. The moment when duality becomes dialogue rather than division.
sections.Shadow: Temptation without discernment. Choice avoided through distraction. The inability to commit because something better might come along. Desire disconnected from values. Betrayal.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_06_Lovers.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes love, union, and choice – the harmony and duality in partnerships and values.

**Upright:** Upright, The Lovers represents partnerships, duality and union. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to explore how you form relationships and make heartfelt choices – aligning decisions with your values and fostering healthy bonds built on honest communication and mutual respect.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The Lovers may signal a loss of balance, one-sidedness or disharmony. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to address imbalances in your relationships or inner conflicts in your values, working to restore honesty, self-love and harmony between the parts of your life that feel disconnected.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/VI_L%27AMOUREUX_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** A decision guided by values—choose what you can stand behind.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** This is about alignment: head, heart, and behavior. If you feel torn, list your values and choose the option that best honors them (not the one that reduces anxiety fastest).

**Reversed:** Avoidance of commitment or self-betrayal. Watch for “shoulds” and people-pleasing. Practice one honest conversation or one honest boundary.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Sesto](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2006.jpg)

**Card name:** Sesto

**Etymology:** Sixth / The Sixth One. From Latin 'sextus' meaning sixth. References Sextus Pompeius, son of Pompey the Great

**Historical figure:** Sextus Pompeius Magnus Pius (67-35 BCE), younger son of Pompey the Great

**Historical context:** Sextus Pompeius was the younger son of Pompey the Great, Rome's most celebrated general before Caesar. After his father's defeat and murder, Sextus became a pirate king, controlling the seas around Sicily and threatening Rome's grain supply. His story is one of loyalty, loss, and the complex choices forced upon those caught in civil war.

The name Sextus means simply 'sixth,' referring to birth order, but the historical Sextus became associated with choice and alliance—he negotiated with various factions, married and divorced for political advantage, and ultimately had to choose between continued resistance and accommodation with the Triumvirate.

Connected by researchers to 'SEXTUS' in Book 6 of Orosius, this card embodies the Lovers archetype's themes of union, choice, and the alignment of values. Sesto reminds us that our choices define us, particularly in times of crisis. The card speaks to partnerships, the harmony or discord between heart and mind, and the sometimes impossible decisions love and loyalty demand.

**Symbolism:** choice, alliance, partnership, values alignment, love and loyalty

sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — L'Amour (The Lovers)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1C1XPiwkFTQF-UyYRvUFZi0O6dCFsvpcm)

**Bolognese name:** L'Amour (The Lovers)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** Two Harlequins meeting at a fountain with Cupid above, representing marriage and union.

**Interpretation:** Marriage, wedding, nuptials, alliance, meeting, link, assembly, pledge, promise, oath, intimacy, liaison, junction, conjunction, copulation, coupling, chain, bondage, discomfort, captivity.

**Reversed:** Union, society, contacts, cohabitation, adultery, incest, alloy, blending, mixing, amalgam, peace, concord, accord, harmony, coexistence, reconciliation, mending, patching things up.

sections.Archetype: Directed will moving through the world. The Chariot doesn't meander — it chooses a direction and moves with the full force of aligned intention. The sphinxes pulling it represent opposing forces (light/dark, conscious/unconscious) held in dynamic tension by the charioteer's mastery.
sections.Professional: Momentum and determination. The Chariot in career says: you know what you want — now go get it. Overcome obstacles through sheer force of will and strategic direction. This is the energy of the product launch, the campaign push, the finishing sprint.
sections.Relationship: Moving a relationship forward through active effort. The Chariot in love says: don't wait for things to happen — create the direction. Plan the trip, have the conversation, make the move. But ensure you're driving together, not dragging your partner along.
sections.Consciousness: The mastery of opposing inner forces — not by suppressing one side but by holding both in creative tension. This is the discipline of meditation: thoughts pull in every direction, and you hold the reins without fighting them.
sections.Shadow: Bulldozing. Steamrolling others in pursuit of your goal. Confusing stubbornness with determination. Road rage of the soul — directed aggression masquerading as purpose. Control that crushes what it touches.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_07_Chariot.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes willpower, direction, and victory – harnessing inner drive to overcome obstacles.

**Upright:** Upright, The Chariot represents direction, control and willpower. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to harness your determination and emotional balance to move forward. It suggests focusing on your goals and confidently steering through challenges by managing conflicting impulses within.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The Chariot may signal a lack of control, lack of direction or aggression. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to examine where you feel pulled in opposing directions or stuck. It’s a reminder to regroup and clarify your goals, addressing inner conflicts or anxieties so you can regain a sense of direction.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/VII_LE_CHARIOT_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Steer your life with intention—two forces can be harnessed.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Pick a direction and commit to daily practice. Motivation follows action. When inner parts conflict, negotiate: what does each part need to cooperate?

**Reversed:** Burnout, aggression, or drifting. If you’re pushing too hard, rest and re-aim. If you’re drifting, define one measurable weekly target.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Deo Tauro](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2007.jpg)

**Card name:** Deo Tauro

**Etymology:** God-Bull / Divine Bull. Likely a corruption of 'Deiotarus' (from Gaulish meaning 'Divine Bull'). References Deiotarus, King of Galatia

**Historical figure:** Deiotarus Philorhomaios (c. 105-40 BCE), King of Galatia, a client king of Rome

**Historical context:** Deiotarus was the ruler of Galatia in Asia Minor, whose name in Gaulish means 'Divine Bull.' He was one of Rome's most important client kings, providing crucial military support during the Mithridatic Wars. Cicero defended him against charges of plotting to assassinate Julius Caesar in the famous oration 'Pro Rege Deiotaro' of 44 BCE.

The 'Divine Bull' etymology connects Deiotarus to ancient fertility cults and the raw power of nature harnessed for civilization. As a client king who maintained his realm through skillful navigation between powerful Roman factions, he embodied the Chariot's themes of triumph through controlled direction of opposing forces.

Researchers have identified 'DEIOTARUS' in Book 6 of Orosius as the source for this card. Deo Tauro represents victory achieved not through brute strength alone, but through the masterful direction of powerful energies. Like a charioteer controlling spirited horses, Deiotarus maintained his kingdom through decades of Roman civil war by channeling competing forces toward his survival.

**Symbolism:** triumph, control of forces, direction, willpower, divine strength harnessed

**Reversed:** Recklessness or excessive caution. Either acting without thinking, or overthinking into paralysis.

sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — Le Chariot (The Chariot)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1xJGojvPmUNaUTskey6NKerNlhQ1cA8pd)

**Bolognese name:** Le Chariot (The Chariot)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** A Harlequin warrior on a chariot pulled by opposing horses, representing conflict and pride.

**Interpretation:** Dissension, discord, disturbances, agitation, noise, nuisance, disorder, riots, insurrection, revolt, sedition, rebellion, defection, faction, conspiracy, war, battle, conflict, combat, duel, violence, impulse, outburst, anger, quarrel, dispute, debate, argumentation, struggle, vengeance.

**Reversed:** Arrogance, pride, vanity, false glory, pomp, ostentation, audacity, temerity, presumption, insult, affront.

sections.Archetype: Courage that comes from compassion, not conquest. Strength is not the warrior's brute force but the calm hand on the lion's jaw — the power to face what is wild and fierce (in the world, in yourself) with gentleness. True strength bends without breaking.
sections.Professional: Quiet persistence through difficulty. Strength in career says: this isn't a problem you can solve with force or cleverness — it requires patience, endurance, and inner fortitude. Lead through calm presence, not loud authority.
sections.Relationship: The courage to be vulnerable. Strength in love says: open your heart even when it's been hurt before. Tame the fierce parts of yourself not through suppression but through compassion. Love the lion; don't cage it.
sections.Consciousness: The integration of instinct and awareness. Strength is what happens when you stop fighting your animal nature and start working WITH it. The tantric path: transforming raw energy into wisdom through acceptance rather than rejection.
sections.Shadow: Repression disguised as composure. 'Keeping it together' at the cost of authenticity. Using gentleness to avoid necessary confrontation. Self-control that becomes self-imprisonment.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_08_Strength.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes inner strength, compassion, and courage – gentle resilience taming the inner beast.

**Upright:** Upright, Strength represents inner strength, bravery, compassion and focus. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to approach your challenges with patience and love. It highlights that true strength often lies in compassionate self-control – calming your inner critic or raw emotions with understanding rather than force.

**Reversed:** Reversed, Strength may signal self-doubt, weakness or insecurity. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to acknowledge vulnerabilities without judgment. It suggests working on rebuilding confidence by treating yourself kindly, and remembering past instances of resilience to counter feelings of inadequacy.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/VIII_LA_JUSTICE_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Reality-testing—what’s true, what’s fair, what’s the next right step?

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Separate facts from interpretations. Make amends where needed, and make decisions you can defend with integrity. Consider: what would be fair to all parts of me?

**Reversed:** Rationalization or denial. If you’re telling a story to protect your ego, gently return to truth. Own one small responsibility today.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Nerone](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2008.jpg)

**Card name:** Nerone

**Etymology:** The Strong One / Brave. From Sabine 'nero' meaning strong, brave, valiant. The cognomen of the Claudii Nerones, most famously Emperor Nero

**Historical figure:** Emperor Nero Claudius Caesar (37-68 CE), fifth Roman Emperor

**Historical context:** Nero remains one of history's most infamous rulers—emperor of Rome from 54-68 CE, remembered for extravagance, persecution of Christians, and the legend that he 'fiddled while Rome burned.' Yet his reign also saw significant artistic patronage and administrative reforms. The truth of Nero is more complex than his dark legend suggests.

The name 'Nero' derives from a Sabine word meaning 'strong' or 'brave,' though this etymology seems grimly ironic given his reputation. According to historian Michael J. Hurst, Nero 'famously blamed the fire on Christians, and tortured them to death as entertainment, being the first ruler to initiate persecution of Christians.'

Identified with 'NERONE' in Book 4 of Orosius, this card embodies the Strength archetype—but not simple physical power. True strength, the card suggests, lies in mastering one's inner beasts. Nero failed this test catastrophically, letting his demons rule him. The card serves as a warning and an invitation: will we master our shadows or be consumed by them?

**Symbolism:** inner strength, mastery over instincts, courage, the shadow self, warning against excess
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):
**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

![Etteilla I — La Force (Strength)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1WaoxgqERGJeFdHhOGci31QOTv9HCDRnh)

**French name:** La Force

**Meaning:** Inner strength, courage, and gentle power.

**Reversed:** Self-doubt or misuse of power. Either not trusting your strength, or using force when gentleness is needed.

**Hermetic correspondence:** Mastery of the animal nature; courage guided by wisdom

**Genesis connection:** Post-Creation: Cardinal Virtue III



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1WA6wUiGj_D5Z7Q8Mnf8dK2AY9D2cXFYr)

*Changes from Edition I: Basan's original engravings replaced with letterpress reproductions. Lower panels redesigned with recycled imagery. Margin titles added to all cards. Published 47 years after Etteilla's death under the pseudonym 'Julia Orsini.'*



**EDITION III — Oracle des Dames (1865, Regamey chromolithographs)**

![Etteilla III](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=19gYIIrO6BnkfxfKKqR-BaSKlBmnFW9Ua)

*Changes from Editions I/II: First colored Etteilla deck. Vibrant chromolithography by G. Regamey for Haugard-Mauge. Marketed as 'Oracle des Dames' (Ladies' Oracle) for middle-class parlor entertainment. Preserves Genesis ordering and Hermetic symbolism in a more visually polished format.*
sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — La Temperance (Temperance)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1ybyurH6eB5MT_ZRjSjgokAp4LZ4__aYT)

**Bolognese name:** La Temperance (Temperance)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** Temperance pouring liquid between vessels, representing moderation and balance.

**Interpretation:** Temperance, moderation, discretion, continence, abstinence, sobriety, frugality, chastity, patience, calm, restraint, accommodation, regard, consideration, mending, mediation, softening, musical tempering, air temperature, climate, temperament.

**Reversed:** Priest, minister, performer of sacrifices, priesthood, clergy, church, council, synod, religion, sect.

sections.Archetype: The lamp in the darkness, carried by one who walks alone. The Hermit has left the marketplace not to escape the world but to find something the world can't give — the inner light that only solitude reveals. This is the archetype of the seeker, the sage, the introvert's deepest gift.
sections.Professional: Step back from the noise. The Hermit in career says: you need time alone to think. The answer won't come from another meeting or more data — it will come from quiet reflection. Take the retreat, close the door, do the deep work.
sections.Relationship: Healthy solitude within partnership. The Hermit in love says: needing time alone doesn't mean you love less — it means you need to refill the well that love draws from. A relationship that can't tolerate solitude can't sustain intimacy.
sections.Consciousness: The contemplative path. Meditation, retreat, the long slow work of self-knowledge. The Hermit's lamp is not for showing others the way — it's for illuminating the path immediately before your own feet.
sections.Shadow: Isolation masquerading as wisdom. Using spiritual seeking to avoid human connection. The hermit who has been alone so long they've forgotten how to come back. Withdrawal as passive aggression.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_09_Hermit.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes introspection, wisdom, and guidance – the quest for truth through solitude and self-reflection.

**Upright:** Upright, The Hermit represents contemplation, a search for truth and inner guidance. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to take time for yourself – to step back from the noise and reflect inward. It suggests that solitude and introspection can provide the insight or answers you seek at this stage of your journey.

**Reversed:** Reversed, The Hermit may signal loneliness, isolation or feeling lost. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to reach out and reconnect where you’ve withdrawn too far. It’s a reminder that while introspection is valuable, support from others and sharing your experience is equally important when you feel adrift.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/VIIII_L%27HERMITE_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Step back to see clearly—quiet reveals what noise hides.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Reduce stimulation. Ask: what’s the essential problem? What’s the essential value? Take one small step with calm consistency.

**Reversed:** Isolation or avoidance disguised as “self-care.” Reconnect to one trusted person or one supportive routine.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Falco](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2009.jpg)

**Card name:** Falco

**Etymology:** Falcon / Hawk. From Latin 'falco' meaning falcon; possibly a corruption of 'Flaccus'. May reference various Romans with cognomen Flaccus (meaning 'flabby-eared')

**Historical figure:** Possibly Lucius Valerius Flaccus or Marcus Fulvius Flaccus, prominent Roman statesmen

**Historical context:** The name Falco connects to the falcon—a bird of keen sight, solitary hunting, and patient observation from great heights. If derived from 'Flaccus,' it may reference several prominent Romans: Lucius Valerius Flaccus, who served with Marius and Sulla, or Marcus Fulvius Flaccus, ally of the Gracchi brothers in their reform movement.

The falcon's symbolism perfectly suits the Hermit archetype: the wise one who withdraws to gain perspective, seeing clearly what others miss from their lower vantage points. The bird represents the soul's capacity to rise above earthly concerns and perceive truth.

Researchers connect this card to 'FLACCUS' in Book 5 of Orosius. Whether we read the name as Falcon or as a Roman historical figure, Falco speaks to the wisdom gained through solitude and observation. The card invites withdrawal not as escape, but as the means to gain the elevated perspective necessary for true understanding. Sometimes we must separate from the crowd to see where it is going.

**Symbolism:** wisdom, solitude, keen observation, elevated perspective, patient watching
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):

**Reversed:** Isolation or avoiding necessary solitude. Either withdrawing unhealthily, or refusing the quiet time you need.

sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — La Justice (Justice)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1vG16syOfBf2Bf_VZk0WleL-C0EgkFzcl)

**Bolognese name:** La Justice (Justice)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** Justice holding scales and sword, representing fairness, law, and moral order.

**Interpretation:** Justice, equity, probity, uprightness, right, natural right, human right, moral right, public right, civil right, rectitude, reason, execution.

**Reversed:** Jurist, legislator, legislation, tribunal, law, decree, code, order, statutes, precepts, commandment, constitution, domination, institution, natural laws, moral laws, religious laws, civil laws, political laws, laws of war.

sections.Archetype: The turning point that no one controls. The Wheel of Fortune is the great leveler — what goes up comes down, what falls will rise, and the only constant is change itself. This is fate, karma, the impersonal cycles of existence. Your job is not to stop the wheel but to find the still point at its center.
sections.Professional: Change is coming whether you plan for it or not. The Wheel in career says: ride the upswings with gratitude and the downswings with grace. Position yourself at the center of change rather than clinging to the rim.
sections.Relationship: Relationships cycle through seasons — growth, stability, challenge, renewal. The Wheel says: this too shall pass, whether 'this' is difficulty or bliss. What remains is the commitment to stay on the ride together.
sections.Consciousness: The meditation on impermanence. The Wheel teaches the Buddhist truth: attachment to any state — even joy — creates suffering. Freedom is found not in controlling circumstances but in witnessing them with equanimity.
sections.Shadow: Fatalism. Using 'it's meant to be' to avoid taking responsibility. Gambling with life instead of engaging with it. Blaming luck for what effort could change.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_10_Wheel_of_Fortune.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes life cycles and change – the turning point of fate inviting adaptation and acceptance.

**Upright:** Upright, the Wheel of Fortune represents change, cycles and inevitable fate. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to recognize what’s within your control and what isn’t. It invites acceptance of life’s ups and downs, reminding you that difficult times give way to better ones, and that adaptability and resilience are key as circumstances evolve.

**Reversed:** Reversed, the Wheel of Fortune may signal feeling no control, clinging to control or bad luck. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to examine your resistance to change. It suggests working on letting go of the need to control every outcome, and reframing setbacks not as personal failures but as temporary phases that you have the strength to get through.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/X_LA_ROUE_DE_FORTUNE_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Seasons turn—adapt rather than clench.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Notice patterns: what repeats? Instead of asking “why me?”, ask “what’s my leverage point?” Choose one adaptive behavior for this phase.

**Reversed:** Resistance to change. If you’re stuck, loosen your grip: change one routine, one thought, or one environment cue.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Venturio](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2010.jpg)

**Card name:** Venturio

**Etymology:** Fortune / The Adventurer. From Latin 'ventura' meaning fortune, chance, or things to come. Possibly connected to Veturius, but etymologically linked to fortune

**Historical figure:** Possibly Veturius from the Roman Republic, or a symbolic figure representing Fortune

**Historical context:** The name Venturio derives from 'ventura'—fortune, chance, the things that will come. This etymological connection to fortune makes the identification with the Wheel of Fortune trump explicit. The concept of Fortuna held immense importance in Roman thought: she was a goddess who could raise the humble and cast down the mighty without warning or apparent reason.

If connected to the historical Veturius (referenced in Book 5 of Orosius), the card gains additional resonance from actual events—the rise and fall of Roman fortunes during the Republic's turbulent final century.

Scholars note that 'Wheel of Fortune = X VENTURIO' creates a perfect alignment of number and meaning. The card embodies the eternal truth that nothing in worldly affairs remains fixed. Kingdoms rise and fall, fortunes reverse, and the wheel continues turning. Venturio reminds us to hold success lightly and bear failure with equanimity, knowing that the wheel will turn again. This is not fatalism but wisdom—understanding that we navigate cycles beyond our control while remaining responsible for our choices within them.

**Symbolism:** fate, cycles, fortune, change, the turning wheel, impermanence
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):
**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

![Etteilla I — La Roue de Fortune (Wheel of Fortune)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1NqeWCQRvIXC0TXfi6tdPBUQeeSRqtnqO)

**Meaning:** Cycles, change, and the turning of fate.

**Reversed:** Fighting the natural cycles or stuck in repetitive patterns. Learn from the cycle rather than repeating it unconsciously.



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1tO2NrCaQoKDWqy9VaY9zQN_A-JJfi-Q1)

*Changes from Edition I: Basan's original engravings replaced with letterpress reproductions. Lower panels redesigned with recycled imagery. Margin titles added to all cards. Published 47 years after Etteilla's death under the pseudonym 'Julia Orsini.'*

![Etteilla III](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1Sile9TkLmjcVMQEnSZKzX3ue39JwFgZ4)

*Changes from Editions I/II: First colored Etteilla deck. Vibrant chromolithography by G. Regamey for Haugard-Mauge. Marketed as 'Oracle des Dames' (Ladies' Oracle) for middle-class parlor entertainment. Preserves Genesis ordering and Hermetic symbolism in a more visually polished format.*
sections.Tarocchino Bolognese:
![Tarocchino — La Force (Strength)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1bvaroZOtiHkuJM07kwBTIfPmqr0TuQz6)

**Bolognese name:** La Force (Strength)

**Commedia dell'Arte reading:** Fortitude embracing a column with baton, representing inner strength and leadership.

**Interpretation:** Fortitude, strength, advantage by strength, ability, power, moral strength, greatness of soul, magnanimity, moral or spiritual work, perseverance, constancy, patience, resignation, heroism, courage.

**Reversed:** Sovereign, kingdom, empire, republic, state, people, nation, government, administration, reign, despotism, sovereignty, authority, leadership, supreme power, absolute power, arbitrary power, king, emperor, general, commander, captain, chief superior, governor, authoritarian, driving force, regulator.

sections.Archetype: The reckoning that cannot be avoided. Justice is not punishment but natural consequence — the universe's way of maintaining balance. What you have sown, you will reap. This card asks not 'are you innocent?' but 'are you honest?' The scales weigh truth, not intention.
sections.Professional: Legal matters, contracts, and accountability. Justice in career says: be scrupulously honest — every shortcut will be found, every promise will be tested. Fairness in negotiations creates lasting partnerships.
sections.Relationship: Accountability in love. Justice asks: are you treating your partner fairly? Are the scales balanced — effort, attention, sacrifice? If something feels unjust, it probably is, and ignoring it won't restore balance.
sections.Consciousness: Karma as teacher, not punisher. Justice in consciousness says: every action has consequences in the inner world as well as the outer. Self-honesty is the prerequisite for every other spiritual virtue.
sections.Shadow: Harsh judgment — of self or others. Legalism without mercy. Using 'fairness' to justify cruelty. The inability to forgive, dressed up as moral clarity.
sections.Rider-Waite-Smith (1909):
![Rider-Waite-Smith](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/RWS_Tarot_11_Justice.jpg)

**Core meaning:** Symbolizes fairness, truth, and accountability – the moral balance of cause and effect in one’s life.

**Upright:** Upright, Justice represents cause and effect, clarity and truth. In a therapeutic context, this card encourages you to take accountability for your actions and to seek truth in your situation. It highlights the importance of honest self-reflection and making amends or adjustments to restore balance and fairness in your life where needed.

**Reversed:** Reversed, Justice may signal dishonesty, unaccountability or unfairness. Through a therapeutic lens, this reversal invites you to consider where you might feel treated unjustly or where you might be avoiding responsibility. It suggests working towards honesty with yourself and others, and understanding that healing often requires addressing imbalances or wrongs rather than ignoring them.
sections.Tarot de Marseille (1760):
![Tarot de Marseille](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/XI_LA_FORCE_Nicolas_Conver_Tarot_1760.jpg)

**Symbolic reading:** Soft strength—regulation beats force.

**CBT-informed interpretation:** Work with your emotions, not against them. Validate the feeling, then choose the behavior. Courage can be slow, kind, and consistent.

**Reversed:** Self-doubt or reactive anger. Practice a pause: breathe, name the feeling, choose a response that matches your values.
sections.Sola Busca (1491):
![Sola Busca — Tulio](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/Sola%20Busca%20tarot%20card%2011.jpg)

**Card name:** Tulio

**Etymology:** Of the Tullii family. From Latin 'Tullius,' an ancient Roman family name. Almost certainly references Marcus Tullius Cicero, the famous orator

**Historical figure:** Marcus Tullius Cicero (106-43 BCE), Rome's greatest orator and philosopher

**Historical context:** Marcus Tullius Cicero stands as Rome's supreme master of rhetoric and one of history's most influential political philosophers. Rising from the equestrian class to the consulship, he exposed the Catiline conspiracy and was hailed as 'Father of the Fatherland.' His writings on law, duty, and the republic shaped Western political thought for millennia.

Art historians at the Brera Gallery note that 'Tulio XI has an uncertain identity,' yet the connection to Cicero seems compelling given the card's themes. Cicero embodied justice in action—he prosecuted corrupt governors, defended the innocent, and articulated principles of natural law that influenced thinkers from Aquinas to the American founders.

Some interpret XI Tulio as 'a skillful defender,' perfectly describing Cicero's role. The card corresponds to Justice—not abstract principle but active engagement with truth and fairness. Cicero was ultimately murdered for his fierce defense of the Republic against Mark Antony. Tulio reminds us that justice often demands courage and may exact a high price, but remains the foundation of civilization.

**Symbolism:** justice, eloquence, defense of truth, law, principled courage
sections.Etteilla (1788–1865):
**EDITION I — Grand Etteilla (1788, Basan engravings)**

![Etteilla I — La Justice (Justice)](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1w11PMUpbNenskp7TiVEsay6gUEYtnIhL)

**French name:** La Justice

**Meaning:** Balance, fairness, and karmic law.

**Reversed:** Imbalance or injustice. Something is unfair—either you're not receiving what you deserve, or you're not giving it.

**Hermetic correspondence:** Ma'at; cosmic balance; the weighing of souls

**Genesis connection:** Post-Creation: Cardinal Virtue I



**EDITION II — Orsini/Blocquel (1838, letterpress engravings)**

![Etteilla II](https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1ZgfhhIOlnSH9n3THJdiEjsYjKTSbFis1)
